front: Novel: a film called 'the Entertainment' is so addictive that viewers lose all interest in anything else; Quebecois Wheelchair Assassins / A.F.R. seek it
Novel: Hal Incandenza memorizes the OED and smokes weed in tunnels at the Enfield Tennis Academy
Novel: 388 endnotes under 'Notes and Errata,' including James O. Incandenza's complete filmography
Novel: Joelle van Dyne (PGOAT) runs radio show '60 min +/-' as 'Madame Psychosis' while wearing a veil
Novel: James Incandenza created the Entertainment to cure his son's anhedonia; committed suicide by sticking his head in a microwave
Novel: Michael Pemulis is the greatest Eschaton player; the game simulates nuclear warfare and degenerates into chaos over the map-territory relation
Novel: Hal is unable to communicate in the chronologically last scene, possibly due to the drug DMZ
Novel: takes place in the 'Year of the Depend Adult Undergarment'; chronology called Subsidized Time
Author: essay about a Caribbean cruise renames the ship Zenith as the Nadir; hypnotist Nigel Ellery puts the narrator in a trance
Author: 'Consider the Lobster,' first published in Gourmet magazine, contemplates ethics of boiling animals at the Maine Lobster Festival
Author: Kenyon College commencement speech opens with two young fish confused by an older fish's question about the title substance
Author: essay traces television's influence on U.S. fiction; praises the 'complicated ironies' in DeLillo's 'most photographed barn' passage
Author: 'Authority and American Usage' calls lexicographer Bryan Garner — who synthesizes descriptivism and prescriptivism — a genius; uses 'SNOOT' in all caps
Author: a 1997 review dismissed John Updike as 'a penis with a thesaurus' and 'Champion Literary Phallocrat'
Author: 'Good Old Neon' — narrator says 'my whole life I've been a fraud'; drives into a bridge abutment; written after death of high school classmate Neal
Author: 'Big Red Son' covers the Adult Video News Awards at Caesars Palace, described as 'as big as 20 Wal-Marts end to end'
Author: Brief Interviews with Hideous Men uses Q-and-A format; includes 'Forever Overhead' (second-person story set at a pool on a boy's 13th birthday)
Novel: unfinished posthumous novel (2011) set at IRS office in Peoria, Illinois; 'Irrelevant' Chris Fogle turns pages and signs Memo 20s; edited by Michael Pietsch
Novel: debut novel; Lenore Beadsman owns a talking bird named Vlad the Impaler and seeks her grandmother who escaped a nursing home
back: The Broom of the System (David Foster Wallace)

front: Novel: publisher Rick Vigorous receives a story about a man with 'second-order vanity' (vain about appearing not vain); Lenore visits her brother 'Antichrist' in Massachusetts
back: The Broom of the System (David Foster Wallace)
